ideas.title: Commemorative Topology: Emergent Scientific Legacy from Gonihedric Dynamics
ideas.description: This research inverts the standard historical analysis by treating the commemorative output for scientists like Einstein, Newton, Darwin, and Maxwell as a complex system. Instead of taking their canonical status as a starting point, it models the 'gonihedric action'—the energy or cost of informational pathways through citation networks, media landscapes, and institutional practices across anniversaries. From the dynamics of this action, a stable, topological invariant akin to the 'Euler character' is predicted to emerge, quantifying their perceived legacy. This allows for a comparative study that explains why commemorative scales differ, not just documents them, by revealing the underlying network mechanics that crystallize a scientist's public and academic stature.

ideas.title: A blinded challenge to benchmark dynamical models for detecting subtle signals in historical Jovian moon data
ideas.description: This project establishes a rigorous, falsifiable framework to assess whether historical observations of Jovian moons contain anomalies beyond known gravitational perturbations. Core historical data is processed into a clean, standardized dataset, which is then used to generate a suite of synthetic observation sets with secretly injected signal types and amplitudes. Independent analysis teams apply and refine dynamical models to these blinded datasets in a competitive challenge, objectively benchmarking the detectability limits of various subtle effects. The final, optimized models and established sensitivity limits are then applied to the genuine historical record, providing a statistically robust conclusion on the presence or absence of anomalous signatures.

ideas.title: Modeling Epistemic Pathways: A Computational Framework for Alternative Histories of Relativity
ideas.description: This project moves beyond speculative narrative by developing a formal, testable model for scientific contingency. It systematically catalogs the late-19th century crisis in physics, quantifying the evidential weight and conceptual challenge posed by each known anomaly to the luminiferous ether. Using agent-based modeling of a simulated scientific community, it runs thousands of counterfactual scenarios to identify minimal, sufficient sets of evidence—excluding the Michelson-Morley result—that could have rationally compelled the adoption of relativity's core postulates. The outcome is a probabilistic map of alternative epistemic pathways, revealing the structural vulnerabilities of the ether paradigm and the conditions under which its overthrow was inevitable or contingent.

ideas.title: Applying Signal Deconvolution Techniques to Historical Jovian Moon Data
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from geophysics to the astronomical problem of re-examining historical anomalies in Jovian moon motion. In seismic signal processing, deconvolution and wavelet analysis are standard techniques for extracting subtle physical signals from noisy, instrument-contaminated, historical time-series data. The adapted idea applies these computational methods to old astronomical records (like those from the 17th-19th centuries) of Jovian moon eclipses and transits. By treating the recorded observational timings as a convoluted signal, we can mathematically separate the known orbital dynamics and characteristic errors of early telescopes and clocks from any consistent residual signal. The goal is to systematically isolate and quantify any subtle, systematic timing anomalies that were previously buried in the data, providing a new quantitative lens to evaluate whether they correlate with potential ether-drift effects or other early tests of relativistic physics.

ideas.title: Hybrid Tensor Network-Monte Carlo Renormalization for Lattice Field Theories with Continuous Variables
ideas.description: This work develops a practical computational framework for systems where the partition function is naturally expressed as a product of high-dimensional or continuous local integrals, such as lattice field theories after a Hubbard-Stratonovich decoupling. The continuous variables are discretized via adaptive quadrature to form a initial tensor network with controlled error. To overcome the bond dimension explosion from the continuous origins, a hybrid renormalization group is implemented where tensor network coarse-graining steps are interleaved with Monte Carlo sampling to prune and approximate the effective tensors. This approach aims to provide a controlled, non-perturbative numerical method for systems where traditional Monte Carlo suffers from sign problems or where pure tensor methods are prohibitively expensive due to large local dimensions.

ideas.title: The Linear Action of a Text: A 2D Field-Theoretic Analysis of a Centenary Volume
ideas.description: This research applies the formalism of a two-dimensional linear action—a simplified field theory describing propagation and interaction in a continuum—to model the intellectual content of a single commemorative book. The text is treated as a discrete 2D manifold (e.g., page number vs. conceptual 'dimension' derived from topic modeling), where the linear action's continuum limit analogizes the smooth flow of argument or narrative across the volume's structure. By analyzing the 'field configurations' that minimize this action, the study quantifies the book's internal coherence, thematic tensions, and rhetorical dynamics, offering a physics-inspired framework for deep textual criticism. This transforms the commemorative volume from a literary case study into a laboratory for testing analytical techniques that bridge formal mathematical structures and humanistic interpretation.

ideas.title: Adapting Quantum Circuit Optimization to Tensor Network Product Representations
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from quantum circuit compilation in quantum computing. There, complex unitary operations are compiled into efficient sequences of hardware-native gates using decomposition and recompilation techniques that eliminate redundancies. We adapt this approach to the analysis of product-of-integrals partition functions represented as tensor networks. By viewing the network as a computational 'circuit' and applying adapted recompilation algorithms (like those based on singular value decompositions and local tensor replacements), we can systematically renormalize the network. This process aims to contract or simplify the tensor product structure, potentially yielding a more compact, computationally tractable representation or identifying dominant contributions to the partition function, similar to how circuit optimization reduces gate depth while preserving the intended quantum operation.

ideas.title: The Gonihedric Text: Reading a Centenary Volume as an Emergent Topology
ideas.description: This research performs a deep, critical reading of a single centenary or commemorative volume, but applies a conceptual inversion from theoretical physics. It treats the book not as a stable, topologically defined object but as a system where meaning is generated through the 'action' of its constituent parts—chapters, images, marginalia, and intertextual references—analogous to the gonihedric model's emphasis on plaquette actions. The traditional expectations of the genre (unity, closure, a definitive 'shape') are then analyzed as potentially emergent, higher-order properties that may or may not stabilize from this underlying textual dynamics. This approach reveals how commemorative meaning is actively constructed and contested within the volume, rather than being a fixed, inherited form.

ideas.title: Quantifying Foundational vs. Revolutionary Contributions: A Network Analysis of Citation and Conceptual Dependence
ideas.description: This research idea applies an analogical solution from the history of biology, where scholars quantified the relative importance of Charles Darwin (revolutionary, unifying theory) and Gregor Mendel (foundational, mechanistic laws) to modern biology. The analogous solution involved network analysis of citations and conceptual dependencies in textbooks and research, and measuring the persistence of core concepts. Adapted to physics, the research will quantify Maxwell's foundational role versus Einstein's revolutionary role by constructing a dual analysis: First, a citation and concept lineage analysis in physics education and review literature from 1905-2025 to map direct dependencies. Second, a formal metric of 'conceptual indispensability' that assesses whether Maxwell's equations or Einstein's postulates serve as more irreducible inputs in the axiomatic structure of modern theoretical physics frameworks (e.g., QED, Standard Model, GR). This moves the debate beyond qualitative historical narrative to a data-driven assessment of foundational vs. revolutionary credit.

ideas.title: Tracing the Epistemic Pathway: A Network Analysis of Correspondence-Inspired Breakthroughs
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution of network contact-tracing, used in epidemiology to map disease spread and in ecology to trace invasive species dissemination, to the historical analysis of Maxwell's letter inspiring the Michelson-Morley experiment. The analogous solution involves modeling the scientific community and Michelson's personal intellectual network as a contact graph. Maxwell's 1879 letter (or the ideas within it) is treated as the originating 'epistemic pathogen' or 'introduced species.' The adapted research idea is to conduct a detailed historical network analysis, tracing the documented transmission of the letter's core challenge—regarding measuring the Earth's motion through the aether—through Michelson's correspondence, reading, conversations, and prior work. This method would move beyond qualitative assertion of influence to a mapped pathway, potentially identifying key intermediaries, the 'incubation period' between receiving the idea and designing the experiment, and the relative influence of this letter compared to other nodes in Michelson's intellectual environment. The goal is to produce a quantifiable model of how a written correspondence directly catalyzed a foundational experimental breakthrough.

ideas.title: A Hermeneutic Feedback System for Validating Computational Patterns in Historical Scientific Correspondence
ideas.description: This redesigned system creates a closed-loop methodology where computational text mining of correspondence (e.g., Maxwell's letters) is strictly framed as a hypothesis-generation engine. Patterns identified by algorithms are automatically formatted as specific historical questions and mapped against a dynamically built ontology of contemporary scientific concepts and social networks. These hypotheses then drive targeted traditional archival research and expert analysis, the results of which are fed back to refine the computational models, preventing anachronistic interpretations. The final output is not a list of mined topics, but a set of contextually validated research threads with explicit supporting evidence from both distant and close reading.

ideas.title: Context-Normalized Analysis of Scientific Commemoration Across Media Regimes
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simple cross-era comparisons by developing a model to quantify and normalize commemorative intensity across different historical media environments. It builds a longitudinal dataset of commemorative events (publications, conferences, institutional namings, media mentions) for a cohort of seminal scientists (e.g., Newton, Darwin, Maxwell, Einstein) spanning from the 18th to early 21st centuries. Using time-series analysis and controls for factors like global scholarly population and communication technology, the model estimates each scientist's 'context-adjusted commemorative footprint.' The output is a robust, era-fair assessment of how commemorative practices reflect scientific legacy, independent of the distorting effects of mass and digital media.

ideas.title: Deep Stratigraphy of a Centenary Text: Applying Core Sample Analysis to a Single Commemorative Volume
ideas.description: This research adapts the core sample analysis method from paleoclimatology to the humanities. Just as geologists extract a single ice core to perform a high-resolution, multi-proxy analysis reconstructing climate history, this project performs a deep stratigraphic reading of one commemorative volume. By treating the book as a cultural core sample, the analysis will examine multiple 'proxies'—including narrative structure, paratextual apparatus, material composition, and traces of use—to map the layered sedimentation of memory, the ideological 'climate' of its production, and the specific historical events fossilized within its pages. This approach scales down to a single case to achieve a vertical depth of analysis that reveals the complex processes of centenary commemoration in microcosm.

ideas.title: Applying Phylogenetic Network Analysis to Historical Scientific Correspondence
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from evolutionary biology—phylogenetic network analysis—to the digital humanities study of historical scientific correspondence. In biology, such networks map complex, non-tree-like evolutionary relationships (like hybridization) between species. The analogous problem in studying correspondence is tracing the non-linear, often conflicting, development and recombination of ideas. The adapted solution involves treating conceptual elements within letters (e.g., 'displacement current,' 'vortex model of the ether') as analogous to genetic traits. By applying network algorithms to correspondence data, the research would generate visual maps of conceptual 'descent with modification,' highlighting how ideas branched, merged, competed, or were abandoned in the informal, epistolary discourse that underpinned formal 19th-century physics publications.

ideas.title: Scale Comparatively: Analyze a Strategic Corpus of Centenary Volumes
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a single case to conduct a comparative discourse analysis of a small, purpose-built corpus of 3-5 commemorative volumes focused on the same centenary event. Volumes are selected to represent key institutional producers (e.g., official state commission, academic press, corporate publisher, civil society group), enabling a systematic investigation of how narrative authority, historical framing, and commemorative ideology vary by sponsor. The analysis identifies both shared tropes and divergent emphases, revealing the political and cultural contestation embedded within centennial publishing and producing findings about the structure of commemorative discourse that are robust and generalizable within the defined context.
